Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I tried to set aside time each day, but I'd often end up studying in bursts when I felt like it rather than sticking to a schedule. Being a kinesthetic learner, I found it hard to just sit and read for hours, so I'd mix studying with walking around or doing something physical. I probably spent about 24 hours a week on coursework, but it was spread out unevenly throughout the week.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was coming up. If there was a deadline approaching, I'd suddenly kick into gear and study more intensely. Otherwise, I'd kind of drift through the week without much urgency. I'm not great at maintaining regular schedules when nothing feels pressing, which I know isn't ideal but it's just how I operate.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I remembered there was something due or when I got a notification. Sometimes I'd log in just out of habit, like checking social media, but then I wouldn't really do much once I was there. If I knew an assignment was coming up, that would be my main reason to actually engage with the platform rather than just browsing around.
Q4: There were a few weeks where logging in became more automatic, especially early in the semester when everything was new. But honestly, that routine faded pretty quickly. Once I figured out where everything was, I'd only go back when I absolutely needed to. It never really became a daily habit for me the way some people seem to manage.
Q5: The video lectures were actually pretty helpful because I could pause and rewind when I needed to. I watched about 80 minutes worth, which helped me understand concepts better than just reading. I also found the peer interaction features useful sometimes because seeing how others approached problems gave me different perspectives on the material.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums entirely. I never posted anything because it felt like a lot of effort for something that wouldn't directly help my grades. Some of the optional readings I also skipped over because I was already struggling to keep up with the required stuff. If something didn't seem immediately relevant to assignments or exams, I'd push it aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my grades or if it helped me understand something I was confused about. The gamified tasks they introduced were actually kind of engaging because they broke up the monotony. But if something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to what I needed to know, I'd lose interest quickly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like extras I couldn't afford to spend time on. Required ones had that built-in pressure, so I'd do them even if I wasn't motivated. With optional stuff, I'd think about it and then decide I had better things to do. There's something about knowing something is mandatory that makes me actually do it, even when I'm bored.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then study from those on my tablet without being connected. I also used my own handwritten notes a lot because writing things out helps me remember better as a kinesthetic learner. Sometimes I'd work through problems on paper rather than doing the online practice modules.
Q10: There were definitely times when I was actively studying but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like when I'd print out materials and spread them across my desk to work through problems physically. Or when I'd study with friends from the course, quizzing each other verbally. None of that hands-on learning shows up in any login data.
Q11: Absolutely, I did this all the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then use it repeatedly over the next few weeks without going back to the LMS. It was just more convenient to have everything saved locally. My LMS activity probably looked way lower than my actual engagement with those specific materials.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got really hard to manage. I was juggling extracurriculars and felt pretty overwhelmed even though my stress wasn't through the roof. The boredom was real too - some of the content just didn't grab me, and when I'm bored, I find it really difficult to push through. My resilience isn't great, so setbacks hit me harder than they probably should.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired or had other responsibilities piling up, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later but then later never really came. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those sections because I didn't want to feel stupid struggling through them alone. It became easier to just focus on what I already understood.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have the LMS open and be scrolling through materials but my mind would be completely elsewhere. Just going through the motions without actually absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd watch a video and realize at the end that I hadn't retained a single thing because I was too distracted or bored to pay attention.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, especially the ones that seemed complicated or boring. Even knowing they were important, I'd find reasons to do other things first. I think my low motivation made it hard to start tasks that didn't have immediate consequences. I'd wait until the anxiety of the deadline outweighed the boredom of the task.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary motivator. My LMS activity probably spiked right before things were due and dropped off in between. Without that external pressure, I'd drift and lose focus. It's not a great system because I'd end up rushing, but it's how I functioned throughout the course. The gamified tasks helped a bit because they created mini-deadlines.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback on my assignments, it did make me feel like engaging more. But if feedback was delayed or vague, I'd kind of forget about it and move on. My assignment scores were pretty good, so that positive reinforcement helped occasionally. Negative or critical feedback sometimes made me want to avoid that area altogether though.
Q18: The course structure was okay but didn't really push me to stay consistently engaged. If there were regular check-ins or smaller milestones, I might have stayed more on track. The way it was set up, I could coast for a while and then cram, which is exactly what I did. More structured pacing might have helped someone like me who struggles with self-motivation.
Q19: The gamified elements actually made engagement easier when they were there. They broke up the monotony and gave me something more interactive to do. But the discussion requirements felt forced and didn't match my learning style at all. I learn by doing, not by typing responses to prompts. More hands-on activities would have helped me stay engaged.
Q20: I think some students might be genuinely trying to understand the material but struggling to produce work that meets the requirements. They could be reading and watching everything but feeling too confused or anxious to actually submit. Or maybe they're perfectionists who keep working on things but never feel ready to turn them in. Activity doesn't always translate to output.
Q21: That actually sounds a bit like me. My exam scores and assignment scores were decent even though I wasn't super active on the LMS. I studied a lot offline and in ways that don't show up in the data. Some people are just more efficient or have different study methods that work for them without needing constant platform engagement.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in and what you click on, which might indicate general engagement trends. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and all the offline work. They can't tell if someone was actually learning or just clicking through mindlessly. The emotional and cognitive aspects of learning are invisible to the system.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just about showing up or clicking through materials - it's about being mentally present and curious. For me, real engagement happens when I'm doing something with the material, not just passively consuming it. It's rare that I feel truly engaged, honestly.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how bored I often felt, or how much I struggled with motivation even when I was technically completing tasks. It wouldn't capture my offline study sessions or the peer discussions I had outside the platform. It also wouldn't show that despite my moderate anxiety, I was actually learning and performing reasonably well. The numbers don't tell the whole story of my experience.
